coredata.dc:description: In practice, nanofluids' thermal conductivity and viscosity are the most important parameters in engineering applications. Viscosity affects pumping performance. Theoretical viscosity correlations are widely used in numerical studies. However, existing correlations show an underestimation of the actual viscosity compared to the measurement results. Although many nanofluid viscosity correlations have been developed, there is no generally accepted correlation. This paper reviews the theoretical, numerical, and experimental viscosity correlations and proposes a new correlation based on an analysis of approximately 1200 experimental and 4000 theoretical data tested for about 50 types of nanofluids in the temperature range 273–333 K and particle diameters 2–300 nm. The studied volume fraction range for the nanofluids was up to 10%. Existing correlations take into account the impact of up to two to three parameters The new viscosity correlation is proposed to predict the effective viscosity of nanofluids based on regression analysis of theoretical and experimental viscosity results, and it considers several factors that significantly affect the effective viscosity of nanofluids, such as nanoparticle diameter, density, temperature, types of nanoparticles, and base fluid.

head.ce:title: A novel empirical equation for the effective viscosity of nanofluids based on theoretical and empirical results

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Miskolc, Institute of Machine and Product Design, Miskolc-Egyetemváros 3515, Hungary

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In practice, nanofluids' thermal conductivity and viscosity are the most important parameters in engineering applications. Viscosity affects pumping performance. Theoretical viscosity correlations are widely used in numerical studies. However, existing correlations show an underestimation of the actual viscosity compared to the measurement results. Although many nanofluid viscosity correlations have been developed, there is no generally accepted correlation. This paper reviews the theoretical, numerical, and experimental viscosity correlations and proposes a new correlation based on an analysis of approximately 1200 experimental and 4000 theoretical data tested for about 50 types of nanofluids in the temperature range 273–333 K and particle diameters 2–300 nm. The studied volume fraction range for the nanofluids was up to 10%. Existing correlations take into account the impact of up to two to three parameters The new viscosity correlation is proposed to predict the effective viscosity of nanofluids based on regression analysis of theoretical and experimental viscosity results, and it considers several factors that significantly affect the effective viscosity of nanofluids, such as nanoparticle diameter, density, temperature, types of nanoparticles, and base fluid.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofluids are a new type of thermal fluids that are produced by dispersing nanoscale particles in a base fluid. Chemical processing, automobiles, air conditioning, solar panel, and power generation are all examples of heat transfer and fluid flow applications.

The homogeneous dispersion of CNTs within polymer matrices has provided enhanced mechanical or electrical properties. It was shown that heat generation has a harmful efficacy on the acute velocity of fluid since it can reduce the acute value due to a reduction in the stiffness of the pipe (see [1]). Hydrodynamic analysis of nanocomposite tubes with internal and external fluid was presented in [2] showing the effect of nanoparticle volume fraction, boundary conditions, length-to-radius ratio, external and internal fluid variables on the dynamic displacement of the piping system.

In various industrial practices, water, oil, and ethylene/propylene glycol are common thermal fluids used in many engineering applications, including power generation, electronics applications, air conditioning, chemical manufacturing processes, heating, cooling operations, nuclear power system cooling, military, transport, and microelectronic applications.

These liquids have poor thermal properties compared to solids. Nanofluids have aroused great interest as they show promising results in improving heat transfer. The properties of fluids are described by four main parameters: viscosity, thermal conductivity, density, and specific heat capacity. Nanofluids' viscosity and rheological properties are extremely important due to thermal and energetic applications. In practice, viscosity is the main cause of pressure drop and pumping performance.

Forced convective heat transfer plays a major role in heat exchange applications with the nanofluid flow. The improvement of forced convective heat transfer can reduce the energy loss and hence scale down the size of a system. The effectiveness of heat exchangers is estimated by the non-dimensional Colburn j factor.

Determining the viscosity of a nanofluid can significantly affect dimensionless and dynamic parameter numbers such as the Reynolds number, the Prandtl number, the Brinkman number, and the Rayleigh number used in numerical analysis studies of thermal and fluid dynamics research. As a result, accurate statistics on the effective viscosity of nanofluids are essential for industrial nanofluid applications. The effective viscosity is influenced by the viscosity of the base fluid and several parameters, we list the most important effects below.
In this section, we shall consider water, ethylene glycol, oil, and some other fluids as the base fluid for nanofluids.

Several studies have suggested water as a base fluid since the addition of nanoparticles to water affects the thermo-physical properties of water. The effect of different nanoparticles on water has been extensively studied; e.g., Nguyen et al. [3] investigated the effect of Al2O3 particle concentration on the dynamic viscosity of water with particle diameters of 36 and 47 nm. They showed that the dynamic viscosity of nanofluids increased significantly from 1% to 9.4% with particle volume fraction, but the temperature decreased with an increase in the range of 295–348 K. The nanofluid volume concentration of TiO2 in water showed a similar result (0.2–2%) [4]. Esfe et al. [5] studied the thermo-physical properties of MWCNT-water, including dynamic viscosity, measured at different temperatures and volume fractions. They showed that increasing the volume fraction of nanofluid increases heat transfer and effective viscosity. Fe3O4 and MgO nanoparticles were also suspended in water, and their viscosity increases with the increasing concentration of nanoparticles (see [6,7]).

Lee et al. [8] examined the viscosity of ZnO-EG nanofluids with particles size less than 100 nm. The results reported that ZnO-EG nanofluids show a Newtonian behavior in the small volume fraction (φ≤0.05) and the effective viscosity increases with increasing particle concentration. Another experiment on the rheological behavior of copper nanoparticles suspended in EG with a volume fraction of 0 to 2% shows that an increase in the volume fraction increases the effective viscosity (see [9]). Esfe et al. [10] showed that for the dynamic viscosity of Fe-EG normalized to the viscosity of an EG-based fluid with a particle size of 35–45 nm at 328 K, an increase in volume fraction results in an increase in effective viscosity.

Thermal oils have a wide range of uses in high-tech industries, such as the automobile industry, due to their excellent cooling and lubricating properties. A review of the most recent research on nanofluid oil base was reported in [11]. Saeedinia et al. [12] studied the rheological characteristics of CuO (50 nm)-oil nanofluid experimentally. They conducted the tests at various solid fractions 0.2–2 particle weight concentrations and temperatures of 293–343 K. They found that the produced samples behaved as Newtonian fluid at the examined temperatures and concentrations. Also, Farbod et al. [13] investigated the rheological properties of CuO-engine oil (20 W50) at various concentrations (0.2–6 wt.), and temperatures 278-343 K. The samples were prepared using CuO nanofluid. They found that the analyzed nanofluid with all particle sizes and engine oil behaved Newtonian over the investigated temperature and concentration ranges. Pakdaman et al. [14] examined the rheological behavior of MWCNT-heat transfer oil inside vertical helically coiled tubes at different temperatures of 313-373 K and three different concentrations of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 particle weight concentrations. They discovered that the shear stress and shear rate of the examined nanofluids have a linear relationship, proving that the nanofluids are Newtonian. Aberoumand et al. [15] investigated the rheological properties of Ag-heat transfer oil nanofluid at temperatures ranging from 298 to 333 K and three-particle weight concentrations of 0.12, 0.36, and 0.72. Their findings were rather intriguing; they found that whereas the heat transfer oil exhibited Newtonian behavior at temperatures below 308 K, the prepared nanofluid exhibited non-Newtonian behavior. On the other hand, the prepared nanofluid displayed Newtonian behavior at temperatures over 308 K. Table 1 summarizes some relevant viscosity correlations for oil-based nanofluid.

The effect of particle concentration and temperature on the viscosity of nanofluids composed of magnetite nanoparticles dispersed in toluene was investigated by Singh [20]. The viscosity of these nanofluids increases with increasing particle concentration. The impact of iron volume concentration (0–0.5%) in a temperature range of 298–338 K was presented, the results showed that the viscosity of nanofluids with 0.5% iron was improved compared to the base fluid, and it is significantly decreased while the temperature increased. Li et al. [21] studied a mixture of 60% water and 40% EG to find the benefits of using two different conventional heat transfer fluids to improve the energy system's performance. They presented experimental data on the viscosity properties of SiC-EG-water (60:40) with a particle size of 30 nm. Their finding for viscosity was that the nanofluid behaves as a Newtonian fluid and the viscosity decreases with increasing temperature. The viscosity of Al2O3-propylene glycol (PG) was investigated in [22]. Experimental results on viscosity at different shear rates, temperatures, nanoparticle diameters, and concentrations have been reported, and it has been found that viscosity increases with increasing particle concentration. Copper oxide nanofluids based on coconut oil have been studied in [23]. It has been reported that the viscosity of nanofluid increases with the loading of nanoparticles and decreases exponentially with increasing temperature. The viscosity of CuO-gear oil nanofluids was studied by Kole and Dey [24]. The effect of CuO nanoparticles on the viscosity of nanofluids in the different volume fractions (0.5–2.5%) and temperature range 283 to 353 K has been shown. The results of the study showed that the viscosity of nanofluids of CuO with a volume fraction of 0.025 improved to about three times that of the base fluid but decreased greatly with increasing temperature.

The most important component of any nanofluid mixture is the concentration of the nanoparticles, which directly affects the effective viscosity of the nanofluids. Several studies have shown that the viscosity of nanofluids increases with increasing particle concentration due to the weight percentage of nanoparticles.

Shear rate is another factor that can affect the viscosity of non-Newtonian nanofluids. Halelfadl et al. [25] experimentally investigated the viscosity of carbon nanotubes in water-based nanofluids. The results showed that nanofluids had a shear-thinning property under high particle loading. Nanofluids behaved similarly to Newtonian fluids at lower particle concentrations. The ratio of the nanofluid viscosity to the viscosity of the base fluid at high shear rates is almost unaffected by temperature. The effect of shear rate on the viscosity of Fe3O4-MWCNTs/EG was investigated by Ahmad et al. [26]. The results showed that low volume concentration nanofluids have Newtonian behavior. Non-Newtonian behavior is observed at higher volume concentrations of Fe3O4-MWCNT/EG. The effect of shear rate on the viscosity of EG-based nanofluids containing ZnO nanoparticles was investigated by Yu et al. [27]. ZnO-EG nanofluids with low volume fractions show Newtonian behavior. At higher volume fractions (greater than 3%), ZnO-EG nanofluids are non-Newtonian.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of morphology and particle size
ce:section.ce:para: Both the shape and size of the nanoparticles can affect the viscosity and pumping power of the cooling system. Chevalier et al. [28] found that the decrease in nanoparticle size increased the effective viscosity. Three different SiO2-water nanoparticle diameters were measured: 35, 94, and 190 nm. The stability and dispersion of nanofluids prepared by dispersing CuO nanoparticles in water have been studied. It has been found that the effect of particle size on density is small but not negligible, but the differences in viscosity are very large and should be taken into account in all practical applications [29].

Temperature is one of the most important parameters influencing viscosity. Based on the experimental results, several correlations have been proposed, but few correlations can be used to measure the approximate viscosity of nanofluids. Table 1 lists the correlations of nanofluid viscosity for different nanofluids derived by the authors.

Based on previous studies, the viscosity of nanofluid increases significantly with increasing particle concentration and decreases with increasing temperature. Nanoparticle types and base fluids affect the viscosity of the nanofluid. Analysis of several experimental studies shows that the theoretical equations consistently underestimate the effective viscosity. There is a big difference between the theoretical and experimental values. Many factors were not taken into account. One is the Brownian motion. Experimental viscosity correlations are currently of limited use.

In the present study, we provide a new correlation for the viscosity of nanofluids using regression analysis based on experimental and theoretical data available in the literature, which can be applied beyond the limits of existing formulas. The current correlations take into account only two or three properties of nanofluids. We want to extend this to volume concentration, temperature, base fluid type and viscosity, nanoparticle size, and density.

Although more and more research is being done on the viscosity of nanofluids (see Table 3), very few studies have focused on developing theoretical correlations for predicting the viscosity of nanofluids, and no correlation is widely available can accurately predict the viscosity. Therefore, researchers often use classical viscosity correlations or empirical correlations based on different measurement data. We first present some classical theoretical viscosity correlations and then correlations based on experimental viscosity data for certain types of nanofluids. Applying these correlations, we develop a new correlation for calculating the effective viscosity of nanofluids.

Among the theoretical formulas used to estimate the particle suspension in the base fluid, Einstein's equation was the first on the rheological behavior of a nanofluid containing dilute, suspended spherical particles with low, 0–2%, volume fraction. The limitation of the correlation is that Einstein did not take into account the interaction of particles in the suspensions. This phenomenon may have a significant effect at a higher volume fraction, and the interaction of the particles may affect the viscosity of the nanofluid. Einstein's formula is written as [40].

Studies on nanofluids have been augmented rapidly in the past decade. In the continuation of these efforts on employing nanofluid in heat transfer performance, many new studies have recently reported using hybrid nanofluid [54,55], which is manufactured by suspending different types of nanoparticles either in mixture or composite form. The novelty of using hybrid nanofluid is for future enhancement of the heat transfer and reducing the pressure drop characteristics through the trading off between the disadvantage and advantage of the individual suspension. Asadi et al. [56] proposed a new correlation to predict the dynamic viscosity of MWCNT-MgO (SAE50) hybrid nano-lubricant in terms of temperature and solid concentration.

In this work, we propose an empirical relationship for the effective viscosity of nanofluid. The new equation for the effective dynamic viscosity of nanofluid, which is normalized to the dynamic viscosity of the base fluid, is developed from a large number of experimental data from the literature. The effective viscosity of the nanofluid is determined by the viscosity of the base fluid, the volume fraction of the nanoparticle, the temperature of the nanoparticle, and the density of the nanoparticles. The new formula is compared with experimental data on nanofluid viscosities in the literature. Various types of nanoparticles such as Al2O3, CuO, Fe, Fe3O4, SiC, ZnO, AIN, TiO2, SWCNT, Zr, Ag, SiO2, CNT, Cu, hBN, CNT, MWCNT, DWCNT, SWCNT, Zr, graphite, CaCO3, and the size of nanoparticles varies between 2 nm and 300 nm in diameter and is suspended in various base fluids, like water, propylene glycol, kerosene, etc. An experimental database of the applied nanofluids is shown in Table 3.
The expected results of the present method (PM) are compared with more than 1150 experimental data, and a high degree of agreement was found. We show that this approximation is more accurate.

In Fig. 1 , the static viscosity calculated is compared with several viscosity correlations. It is observed that the gap in predictions of the effective viscosity depending on the volume fraction is big. Where as can be seen, the ratio can be varied from 1 to more than 10. The result of the proposed static viscosity is to bridge the gap in modeling the nanofluid viscosity. The equation is written in power form (see (31)).

It is evident that the viscosity of nanofluid is influenced by many factors, including volume concentration, temperature, and nanolayer thickness, as well as geometrical parameters of particles (size and shape), aspect ratio, and interparticle spacing. The effects of nanofluids on the viscosity of nanofluids have been studied in terms of electromagnetic fields, electro-viscosity, dispersion energy, settling time, and the effects of base fluid parameters such as density and polarity. Our research focuses on the six most significant properties: particle size, volume concentration, base fluid, particle density, base fluid viscosity, and temperature.

The comparison between the experimental results and the prediction of different theoretical correlations shows an underestimate of the viscosity. Examples of the gap between the experimental result and the existing theoretical correlations are presented in Figs. 2-20
. The effect of the type of the nanoparticle and base fluid is investigated. The errors between the relevant experimental data and the present correlation (35) are determined.

The large number of papers studied show that no viscosity correlation can provide the effect of broad types of nanoparticles on the viscosity of nanofluids. A comparison of different nanoparticle types and different base fluids shows that the viscosity of the nanofluid is affected by the density of the nanoparticles. It has been found that the viscosity of nanofluid increases with the increasing density of nanoparticles. In (35), the presence of nanoparticle's density appears only in the Brownian viscosity, which is proportional to
ρ p
.

On the one hand, the proposed correlation for different nanoparticle types was analyzed, and the viscosity values were compared with the experimental data in Table 4 in the relevant volume fraction to examine the suitability of the correlation for different nanoparticle types. On the other hand, the presence of the effect of the base fluid on the effective viscosity is as follows:
μ nf
=
μ static
+
μ Brownian
μ bf
.
Hence, the viscosity of the base fluid directly affects the effective viscosity. Thus, as the viscosity of the base fluid increases, so does the effective viscosity of the nanofluid.

The experimental result [25] for the steady-state viscosity of CNT in water with an average diameter of 9.2 nm, for 0.0055% and 0.55% of the particle fraction, reported that the fluids were Newtonian at low particle concentrations. A comparison of the present method (PM) given by eq. (35) and other viscosity correlations with the experimental results is shown in Fig. 2 at a temperature of 293.15 K and a particle size of 9.2 nm. The maximum error is less than 1%.

Experimental determinations of the viscosity for Fe3O4-water [6] measured in the temperature range of 293 K to 328 K for different volume fractions of 0.1%, 0.2%, 0.4%, 1%, 2%, and 3% showed that the viscosity significantly decreased with increasing temperature. In addition, the viscosity increases significantly with increasing solids volume fraction. Using experimental data, the calculated viscosity ratios showed that the maximum viscosity increase was 129.7% at a volume fraction of 3.0% and a temperature of 328 K. A comparison of the experimental results with the proposed correlations showed that the current correlation has excellent accuracy with a maximum error of up to 2% (see Fig. 3).

Turgut et al. [75] investigated the viscosity of TiO2 nanoparticles in deionized water up to 3% concentration. Nanofluids were prepared by dispersing TiO2 nanoparticles in water using ultrasonic devices. The nanoparticles had an average diameter of about 21 nm, and the viscosity was measured at 286, 296, 313, 328 K. The experimental results reported an increase in the viscosity of the nanofluid with an increase in particle volume fraction and stated that the increase in viscosity was much larger than predicted from the Einstein eq. A comparison of the proposed correlation and the results of [75] is shown in Fig. 4 at a particle size of 286 K and 21 nm. The maximum error did not exceed 4%.

Fig. 5 presents the measured data of the dynamic viscosity of Zr-water normalized to the viscosity of the water base as a function of volume fraction at a particle size of 10 nm at 303 K [65] and calculated by the proposed correlation. The maximum error was below 4%.

In [83], freeze etching replication transmission electron microscopy (FERTEM) was used to characterize the microstructures of CaCO3-water nanofluids at different particle volume fractions. It was concluded that the effective viscosity of CaCO3-water nanofluids is related to aggregates of nanoparticles. The viscosity of CaCO3-water is well predicted by the proposed correlation, as shown in Fig. 6, with a maximum error of 3%.

Experimental work [5] was performed to investigate the viscosity of COOH-functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes with a water-based liquid using 22 concentrations as a function of temperature. The main result shows that the increase in the volume fraction increased the viscosity of the water. The comparison of the authors' findings in [5] and the results of the proposed correlation is presented in Fig. 7. The maximum deviation is less than 2%.

To research the thermo-physical properties of nanofluids, Dalkilic et al. [74] produced graphite-pure water nanofluid with a particle size of 8 nm and a volumetric concentration ranging from 0 to 0.02 for temperatures between 293 K and 333 K for stabilized nanofluid produced with a sonicator and an ultrasonic bath. The authors presented an interesting comparison using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) analysis with 32 empirical correlations. The variation of the viscosity correlations compared to the experimental result was between 3.507 and 127.151%. However, the maximum error with the proposed correlation is 5% in Fig. 8 for graphite water with particle size 8 nm at 333 K.

The effective viscosity of SiO2-water nanofluid is determined from 0 to 5% in the work [28]. Fig. 9 shows a comparison of the experimental work and the proposed correlation. The maximum error was less than 8%.

Khodadadi et al. [72] studied the rheological behavior of MgO dispersed in water nanofluid at various temperatures of 298–333 K and particle concentrations between 0.07% and 1.25%. The data obtained also showed that the viscosity increases with increasing volume fraction and decreases with increasing temperature. In Fig. 10, the experimental result for the viscosity of MgO-water with a particle diameter of 20 nm at 298 K is compared with the proposed correlation, and the result shows an excellent agreement (<3%) using the correction factor.

Mena et al. [71] presented experimental data for the nanofluid viscosity of Al2O3-water with a particle diameter of 20 ≤ d
p ≤ 30 nm, volume concentration of 0.5–4%, and a temperature between 283 K and 298 K. A comparison of the viscosity correlations with the experimental result is shown in Fig. 11. The maximum error between the experiments and the present correlation was less than 5%.

The stability of nanofluids prepared by dispersing Al2O3 nanoparticles in ethylene glycol at various concentrations up to 25% by weight was studied in [69]. At temperatures between 283.15 K and 323.15 K, the viscosity was evaluated experimentally. Viscosity increases with the increasing concentration of nanoparticles, while viscosity decreases with increasing temperature. The results of this experiment were compared with the results of the proposed correlation as shown in Fig. 12. The maximum error was less than 5%.

Copper nanoparticles dispersed in EG were prepared by a chemical reduction method. The nanoparticles were then dispersed in EG using a sonicator with a volume ratio of up to 2%. Viscosity measurements show four times higher values than predicted by Einstein's correlation (see [9]). The error did not exceed 2% with the formula (35) we calculated. Fig. 13 depicts the proposed correlation predictions against experimental data for Cu-EG with a particle size of 200 nm at 298 K.

Various ZnO-EG nanofluids have been prepared using ZnO nanoparticles dispersed and synthesized in EG [78]. The result reported that the increase in volume fraction increased the interactions between nanoparticles and had an effect on volumetric trends. The rheological behavior was Newtonian, and the viscosity increased with concentration and decreased with temperature. Fig. 14 represents the viscosity of ZnO-EG nanofluids at 100 nm and 293 K [78]. The error is below 2%.

Akbari et al. [33] studied the viscosity of SiO2 as a function of temperature and concentration. Experiments were performed on SiO2 with a volume fraction of 0.1–3% and a temperature range of 303 K to 323 K. According to the experimental results, all nanofluid samples behaved as Newtonian fluids at all applied temperatures. Viscosity measurements show that the viscosity increases with an increasing volume ratio of nanoparticles and decreases with increasing temperature, with a maximum increase of 116% of the viscosity at 3% concentration. The application of the proposed correlation to predict the viscosity of SiO2-EG (20–30 nm) at 313 K is shown in Fig. 15. The maximum error is less than 4%.
Fig. 16 shows the viscosity of hexagonal boron nitride (hBN)–EG nanofluids with a particle size of 70 nm at 298 K [76]. The proposed correlation shows excellent accuracy of less than 1%.

Fig. 17 illustrates the dynamic viscosity of Fe normalized with EG-based fluid. With the proposed predictive correlation. The dynamic viscosity as a function of volume fraction was calculated and compared with the measurement points (35–45 nm) of Fe-EG nanofluids at 328 K [10]. The maximum error was below 4%.

A mixture of 60% water and 40% EG has been investigated to find the benefits of using two different conventional heat transfer fluids to improve energy system performance. In the work [21], experimental data on the viscosity properties of SiC-EG water (60; 40) with a particle size of 30 nm and volume fractions of 0.002, 0.004, 0.006, 0.008, 0.01 were presented.

The finding of the authors was that nanofluid behaves as a Newtonian fluid and the viscosity decreases with increasing temperature. A comparison of the measured viscosity result with the proposed correlation is shown in Fig. 18 at 283 K, where the maximum error did not exceed 2%.

Fig. 19 depicts the viscosity of Fe3O4-toluene at 293 K with a particle size of 10 nm as described in [20] with different particle concentrations. The viscosity of the nanofluid decreases with temperature, which is comparable to the properties of the carrier fluid. A comparison of (35) with the experimental results shows a good agreement. The maximum error was less than 3%.

The dispersion viscosity behavior of SiC-DIW nanofluids as a function of volume fraction was studied in [73]. A comparison of the proposed correlation and the experimental results is shown in Fig. 20. The maximum error was less than 5%.

In Fig. 21 . The result on the viscosity ration for stable CuO-oil nanofluids with different particle weight fractions are presented [12]. The comparison with the experimental result at 303 K reported a maximum error of 4%.

In the following, we analyze the impact of temperature on the viscosity of the nanofluid and examine the accuracy of the correlation (35) to predict the viscosity of the nanofluid. Experimental results on the effect of temperature on dynamic viscosity show that an increase in the temperature of the nanofluid leads to a decrease in the dynamic viscosity, and the declination of the dynamic viscosity can be attributed to a factor such as the motion of nanoparticles on a micron scale. Studies show that the micro-convection of nanoparticles in the base fluid reduces intermolecular forces between the base fluid molecules. The results of the application of the proposed correlation also showed that with increasing temperature, the dynamic viscosity of nanofluid decreases significantly. As the temperature increases, the molecular velocity of the nanoparticles increases due to the Brownian motion of the nanoparticles [84]. Increasing the random velocity of the nanoparticles leads to a decrease in the intermolecular forces between the base fluid and the surface of the nanoparticles, resulting in higher viscosity at lower temperatures.

Viscosity change of the nanofluid with temperature was investigated according to the correlation (35) and the accuracy of the proposed correlation was validated with measurement data. In the proposed correlation, the last term is strongly influenced by temperature, and the numerator is proportional to
T
and in the denominator the correction factor C(T, φ) = D
1
T −B 1
+ D
2
φ −B 2 is influenced with T −B 1 and μ
bf also depends on T.

As the temperature increases, the viscosity of the base fluid decreases. Two parameters, the viscosity of the base fluid and the Brownian viscosity affect the viscosity of the nanofluid. The nanofluid viscosity of CuO-water was determined according to the method of [29] with a particle size of 11 ± 3 nm at the following temperatures: 283.15, 288.15, 293.15, 298.15, 303.15, 308.15, 313.15 K, and 323.15 K. Fig. 22 shows a comparison of CuO-water at different temperatures and concentrations, and the comparison shows a good agreement between the published measurement results and the proposed correlation. The maximum error was less than 5%.

Nanofluids prepared by dispersing Al2O3 nanoparticles in ethylene glycol have been tested at various concentrations up to 25% in a mass fraction [69]. At temperatures between 283.15 K and 323.15 K. Viscosity was determined experimentally with a rotational viscometer. As the concentration of nanoparticles increases, the viscosity increases, but as the temperature increases, the viscosity decreases. The new experimental viscosity data values are more than twice that of the base fluid. A comparison of the experimental results of Al2O3-EG with a diameter of d
p = 43 ± 23 nm is illustrated in Fig. 23 .

Several researchers have published conflicting results regarding the relationship between temperature, nanoparticle diameter, nanoparticle volume concentration, and nanofluid viscosity. Mena et al. [71] attempted to obtain an approximate expression for the systematic evaluation of the viscosity of Al2O3-water in the simulation of heat transfer applications in the temperature range of 283-298 K and nanoparticle volume fractions below 1%. Their work presents a methodology for extrapolating a correlation in the validity range. Within this narrow temperature and volume percentage range, new experimental data were collected for the nanofluid viscosity of Al2O3-water. A comparison of the experimental data with the proposed Al2O3-water correlation for 20 < d
p < 30 nm diameter shows an excellent agreement, as shown in Fig. 24 .

Based on experimental data in [74] of graphite water with a diameter of 6–10 nm at 293–333 K, it was found that the dynamic viscosity of nanofluid increases with decreasing temperature and increasing concentration. Fig. 25 shows a comparison of the present correlation with the experimental results. The maximum error was less than 5%.

Fig. 26 shows the viscosity of Fe3O4 water with a diameter range of 20–30 nm at different temperatures and a comparison of the present correlation with experimental results [6], which gives excellent agreement.
Furthermore, the validation of the correlation with the experimental result [33] of SiO2-EG with a particle size of 20 nm is shown in Fig. 27 . The maximum error was less than 5%.

The diameter of the nanoparticles has a significant effect on the viscosity of the nanofluid, which requires an examination of the accuracy of the correlation for predicting the viscosity ratio and viscosity of the nanofluid. Examining the accuracy of the proposed correlation for the viscosity of nanofluids of different diameters containing Al2O3, CuO, Fe, Fe3O4, SiC, ZnO, AIN, TiO2, SWCNT, Ag, SiO2, MWCNT, DWCNT, SWCNT, CaCO3, hBN, MgO and graphite, our analyses show excellent agreement.

Pastoriza-Gallego et al. in [29] studied the nanofluid viscosity of CuO-water. The viscosity of two different samples was experimentally measured. The researchers examined two separate samples, the first S1 with commercially available dry CuO nano-powder and the second S2 with synthesized CuO nanoparticles prepared by a precipitation method. The average diameter of S2 nanoparticles is smaller than that of S1, and the particle size distribution of S2 is narrower than that of S1. Fig. 28 presents the sensitivity of the correlation to particle size based on the experimental results of the two samples. According to the experimental result, the increase of the particle size significantly affects the viscosity of the nanofluid, and the viscosity decreases with the increase of the particle size (see Fig. 29.)

Pastoriza-Gallego et al. [69] reported experimental results for another Al2O3-EG test for two samples with diameters S1 = 8 ± 3 nm and S2 = 43 ± 23 nm. The effect of this change in diameter is shown in Fig. 30 . A comparison with the proposed correlation shows an excellent agreement.

The current methods for producing nanoparticles produce nanoparticles with different diameters. Fig. 31 illustrates the effect of particle size ranges on viscosity prediction. The viscosity of the nanofluid increases compared to the base fluid. On the other hand, the particle size affects both the static and dynamic viscosity parts. The proportion of the part from the Brownian motion is significantly larger at small diameters than at larger particle sizes. It can be observed that the viscosity of the nanofluid increases with decreasing particle size. We found that the use of the average diameter is the most accurate in the current correlation.

The discovery of nanofluid has sparked interest in its use as a new type of thermal fluid in engineering applications. Therefore, experiments are needed to evaluate the thermophysical parameters of nanofluids that affect heat transfer, such as specific heat capacity, viscosity, thermal conductivity, and density. In the numerical analysis, CFD simulations of engineering applications, theoretical relationships are used to calculate thermophysical parameters. Our research aims to create a new correlation for determining the effective viscosity of nanofluids that takes into account the effect of several parameters than the available viscosity correlations and approximates the measurement results more accurately.

A comparison of the experimental results with the existing correlations shows an underestimation of the viscosity. Examples of the discrepancy between the experimental result and the existing relationships are shown in Fig. 31. Based on our analysis, the current correlation (35) successfully predicts approx. Fifty viscosities of nanofluids in different diameter ranges. The diameter range was between 3 nm and 200 nm in each case.

The size of the nanolayer has been investigated in [85], where Yu and Choi demonstrated the effect of the assumption of the nanolayer thickness h. As there is currently no method for estimating h, we determined it by fitting the experimental data. For a spherical shape, h equals 1 nm, and for a nanotube, h = 2 nm. However, the error does not exceed 5% for any of the MWCNT water nanofluids (5–10 nm) at 293 K [5]. Comparison of the nanolayer thickness at h=1 nm and h=2 nm for graphite-water with a particle size of 10 nm at 333 K showed that the effect of the assumption was negligible for a small volume fraction and gradually increased with increasing volume ratio. The effect of the nanolayer assumption on the correlation accuracy was also investigated for different spherical nanoparticles at different temperatures. A comparison of CuO-water with a particle size of 45 nm at different temperatures was performed with the results published in [29]. Our studies showed that the effect of the nanolayer's size on the partly large particles was negligible.

The assumed effect of the nanolayer on the prediction of MWCNT-water nanofluids (5–10 nm) at 293 K was compared with [3] at different volume fractions. The assumption h=2 overestimated the experimental result, while h=1 underestimated it. The error was less than 2% for both cases.

The study of nanofluids' effective viscosity consists of static and dynamic viscosity, and a novel viscosity correlation is proposed. Static viscosity is a combination of different theoretical and experimental correlations, and the viscosity is affected due to the nanolayer. The concept of the impact of a nanolayer is that a nanolayer is formed around a particle and has a thickness of about 1 nm. The dynamic part is due to the Brownian motion of the particles. We proposed a correction factor of two power forms with four parameters for the Brownian motion viscosity. The parameters are given in Table 5 and are independent of the type of the base fluid.

Surfactants and dispersants are commonly used to stabilize nanofluids. The use of surfactants lowers the surface tension of base fluids and allows particles to be immersed. Surfactants are commonly employed to keep nanofluids stable [11]. Kaggwa et al. [86] investigated the effect of surfactants on nanofluid viscosity. This is the first work to address the effect of surfactants on the measurement of nanofluid viscosity. The authors revealed that further experimental work is needed to better understand the micelle formation of different surfactants with nanofluids and to select the optimal pairing of surfactants, nanoparticles, and base fluids. The current correlation is based on the assumption that nanofluids are homogeneous suspensions with good stability, and no sedimentation was observed in the long-time term.

The correction factor is determined using only a few experimental data for nanofluids produced by a one-step method. However, most of the data used were prepared using a two-step method. In most experimental studies published in the literature, only two or three key parameters were examined, whereas we included six parameters.

It is recommended that researchers conduct further experiments in the future to take into account additional key factors. These include the effects of preparation methods, stability, non-Newtonian properties, geometrical parameters of the particles, scale to the distance between the particles, electromagnetic fields, electro-viscosity, dispersion energy, settling time, and basic fluid parameters such as density and polarity. Knowing these would lead to more accurate correlations to predict the effective viscosity of nanofluid.

Nanofluids have a wide range of properties due to non-Newtonian rheological characteristics, for example. The present proposed correlation underestimated these nanofluids at high shear stress for nanofluids showing shear thinning, which limited the validity of the proposed correlation for Newtonian nanofluids. However, the proposed correlation has acceptable accuracy at low shear stress rates. The effect of shear stress greatly affects the viscosity, as it was shown in [11]. Determining the relationship between shear rate and correlation factor will be further investigated. We would like to develop further the current method for hybrid nanofluids with non-Newtonian rheological properties.

A novel analytical correlation was given to determine the effective viscosity of a fluid containing homogeneously dispersed rigid and spherical nanoparticles. The effective viscosity of nanofluids consists of two parts, the static and dynamic or Brownian viscosity. The static part of the viscosity of the nanofluid is a combination of the regression analysis performed in the theoretical and experimental correlation and the viscosity effect due to the nanolayer. A correction factor was derived as a function of temperature and volume fraction. The parameters in the correction factor were determined using several experimental results, and the correction formula was tested with about 50 different nanofluids. Approximately 1200 experimental viscosity values were used to test the new correlation, 87% within the 0.9–0.99% correlation coefficient.
